description: Someone is 'nukesa' when they have deep knowledge and skill in a stated domain, built through sustained practice and study. An old chair maker who can read a warped board at a glance may be expert without being 'kiranu' (qualified) under a new certification scheme. 'pokera' (competent) meets a present criterion; expertise covers a wider body of practice and understanding.
articulatory_notes: Place the tongue tip against the backs of the upper teeth for voiced dental 'n', carry the voice through the nose, and finish the first syllable with high rounded back 'u'. Close the back of the tongue against the soft palate for unvoiced 'k' and release it into stressed true-mid front unrounded 'e'. Raise the tongue blade towards the alveolar ridge for unvoiced 's', direct air through the narrow channel, and open into central 'a'.
usage_notes: Context supplies the domain and basis of the claim. As a noun, 'nukesa' is expertise. Decision power belongs to 'karami' (authority) or a bounded 'kanuwa' (authorize) relation.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: An expert can be questioned without pretending their practice is shallow; the word honours knowledge and leaves decisions open.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: A grower who has watched one slope through forty winters may hold expertise that no certificate records.

semantic_domains.activity: deeply practised capacity within a stated domain
semantic_domains.cognition: extensive domain understanding supported by practice and study
semantic_domains.wisdom: understanding refined through long practice and reflection
